title: Romesco Sauce
subtitle: Catalan roasted pepper and almond sauce
excerpt: Romesco is a Catalan sauce of roasted red peppers, almonds, hazelnuts, garlic, and sherry vinegar blended into a thick, smoky, brick-red paste. It pairs with grilled meats, fish, vegetables, and eggs. The sauce keeps in the fridge for a week.
sourceNotes: Romesco sauce originates in Tarragona, Catalonia, where it is the traditional accompaniment to calcots (grilled spring onions) at the annual Calçotada festival. The sauce also appears throughout the year with fish, grilled meats, and vegetables across Catalonia.

recipe.batchNotes: Keeps in the fridge for 7 days in a sealed jar.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made a day ahead; the flavour develops.

content.text: Romesco is a thick, rust-coloured Catalan sauce of roasted peppers, nuts, garlic, and vinegar. It has a smoky sweetness from the peppers, a nutty richness from the almonds and hazelnuts, and a clean sharpness from the sherry vinegar. It is one of the most versatile sauces in Spanish cooking: it works with almost anything grilled or roasted.

items.prepNote: roasted, skin and seeds removed (or 3 jarred roasted peppers)
toasted in a dry pan until golden

content.text: Toast the almonds and hazelnuts in a dry frying pan over medium heat for 3 to 4 minutes until golden and fragrant. Rub the hazelnuts in a clean cloth to remove most of the skins. Leave to cool.

Place the roasted peppers, toasted nuts, garlic, tomato, sherry vinegar, olive oil, smoked paprika, and cayenne if using in a food processor. Blitz until roughly combined. The sauce should be thick and slightly textured, not completely smooth.
Taste and adjust: more vinegar for sharpness, more olive oil for richness, more salt for seasoning. Transfer to a jar and refrigerate. The sauce thickens as it chills.

items.cause: Too much olive oil for the nut content
items.fix: Add more nuts and blend again. The nuts absorb the oil and give body to the sauce.

items.cause: Hazelnuts not toasted enough, or too much raw garlic
items.fix: Toast the nuts until properly golden; pale nuts are bitter. Use 2 garlic cloves instead of 3 for a milder result.

content.text: Romesco is the sauce of Catalan cooking. It appears at the Calçotada, the spring festival of grilled spring onions, where it is provided in vast quantities for dipping. It is also used as a sauce for grilled fish, chicken, lamb, and roasted vegetables, and as a dip for bread. A jar in the fridge is one of the most useful things to have on hand.
